hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Talking About Your Work -- Explaining Your Role and Industry with Confidence
lesson.menu_desc: Da feira de Lyon a um jantar em Nova York: como explicar sua empresa e sua industria em ingles simples -- passive voice (are tested, are set, was approved) e o vocabulario de negocios e regulatorio -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- Talking About Your Work
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 10 -- Talking About Your Work

listenings.text: Good afternoon, Ms. Ito, this is Daniel Lefebvre. We met at your booth at the Lyon fair last week, and I wanted to follow up. I was very impressed by what I saw. My company distributes specialty chemicals across France and Belgium, and we are always looking for new partners whose products are made to a high standard. What caught my attention was one thing you said: that every batch is tested before it is released, and that your plant is certified to international standards. That matters to us, because in our market a product without proper certification simply cannot be sold. So here is what I would like to propose. If your raw materials are sourced responsibly and your supply chain can reach Europe, I believe we could become your distributor for the region. Nothing is decided yet, of course. But the first shipment could be arranged for early next year, once the paperwork is reviewed on both sides. Please send me your company profile and your compliance documents when you have a moment. You explained everything so clearly at the fair, in English, and that is exactly the kind of partner we want. I look forward to hearing from you. Thank you, Ms. Ito.

Welcome back to The Global Table, the show for people who do business across borders. Today, one skill that is worth more than perfect grammar: explaining what you do without getting lost. Here is the trap. You are an expert. You know your industry inside out. So when someone at a dinner asks, what do you do, you either say three boring words, I work in chemicals, or you drown them in detail they did not ask for. Neither one works. The people who do this well follow a simple pattern, and you can steal it tonight. First, one sentence for the field: our company specializes in specialty chemicals. Second, one sentence for the value, and this is where the passive voice becomes your best friend, because in business we care about the process, not the person. Our products are tested before they are shipped. Regulations are set by the health authority, and we follow every one of them. Notice how you never have to say who tests, or who sets the rules. The process is the star. Third, and only if they seem interested, one human detail: it is a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials that other factories cannot make safely themselves. That is it. Field, value, human detail. Three sentences, and you sound like someone who knows exactly what they do, because you do. The goal was never to say everything. The goal is to be understood.

paras: The fair in Lyon was held every November, and for years it had been the same quiet dread. Lucimara would stand at her company's booth, hand out brochures, and hope that no one stopped to ask the one question she could never answer in English: "So, what exactly does your company do?" She knew the answer perfectly in Portuguese. She was a director. She ran production and regulatory affairs for a chemical company in Guarulhos. But every time the question came in English, the words were scattered, and she would smile and point at the brochure instead of speaking.

This November was different. A man stopped at the booth, read the sign, and asked the question. This time she did not point. "We specialize in specialty chemicals," she began. "Our products are manufactured in Guarulhos, near São Paulo, and every batch is tested before it is released." She watched his face. He was still listening. "The whole manufacturing process is monitored by our quality control team, and the plant is certified to international standards." She had said it. Three sentences, in English, and the man was nodding, not because he was being polite, but because he had understood.

"Interesting," he said. "And your supply chain? Where are the raw materials sourced?" It was exactly the kind of question she had always feared, and now it felt easy, because she knew the words. "Most of our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe," she answered. "Each shipment is checked when it arrives, and nothing enters the manufacturing process until it is approved." She was not reciting a script. She was explaining her own company, the one she ran, in a second language, to a stranger who might matter.

The man handed her his card. His name was Daniel, and his company was a distributor of specialty chemicals across France and Belgium. "In our market," he said, "a product cannot be sold without proper certification and full regulatory compliance. From what you have told me, yours has both." He was right. Every regulation in her sector was set by the health authority, and she had spent her whole career making sure they were followed. Two minutes, one language, and a booth she used to dread. The director from São Paulo had just explained her entire company in English, and someone in Lyon had listened, understood, and asked for more.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The Lyon fair is too stressful, and Lucimara should stop attending it every November.
Explaining your company is not about knowing every technical word. It is about giving a clear, confident answer -- field, then process -- so a stranger actually understands what you do.
Daniel could not understand anything Lucimara said, so he asked her to send a brochure instead.

items: For years, Lucimara answered the "what does your company do?" question easily in English.
"every time the question came in English, the words were scattered, and she would smile and point at the brochure instead of speaking." She dreaded it -- this year was the first time she answered.
Lucimara explained that every batch is tested before it is released.
"Our products are manufactured in Guarulhos... and every batch is tested before it is released." That is the passive voice: the process is the star, not the person.
The raw materials come only from Brazil.
"Most of our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe." A vendor is a company that sells goods to another company.
In Daniel's market, a product can be sold even without certification.
"a product cannot be sold without proper certification and full regulatory compliance." Certification is the official proof that a product meets a standard.
Daniel's company is a distributor of specialty chemicals in France and Belgium.
"his company was a distributor of specialty chemicals across France and Belgium." A distributor buys products and sells them on to stores or clients.

items.situation: At a dinner in New York, someone asks, "So what do you do?" Give the one-sentence version -- your field, nothing more yet.
tips: I'm a director at a chemical company. We specialize in specialty chemicals.
Start with the field in one clean sentence. You can always add more if they seem interested -- don't drown them in detail they didn't ask for.

items.situation: They want to know if your products are safe. Use the passive voice -- the process is the star, not the person.
tips: Every batch is tested before it is released, and the whole process is monitored by our quality control team.
'is tested', 'is monitored' -- be + past participle. You never have to say WHO tests it. In business, the process matters, not the actor.

items.situation: A potential distributor asks where your raw materials come from. Answer with the passive.
tips: Most of our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe.
'are sourced' -- passive, present. The focus is on the materials and the supply chain, not on who does the buying.

items.situation: Explain, in the passive, that your factory follows the rules set by the regulator (Anvisa).
tips: Regulations are set by the health authority, and every one of them is followed.
'are set by the health authority' -- here you DO name the actor with 'by', because who sets the rules matters. Use 'by' only when the actor is important.

items.situation: Tell a partner that last year's formula was officially approved. Use the PAST passive.
tips: The new formula was approved last year, and the certification was renewed in the spring.
'was approved', 'was renewed' -- past passive is was/were + past participle. A finished action, and again the process is the star.

items.situation: Someone looks a little lost by the technical detail. Soften it with one human sentence.
tips: It's a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials that other factories can't make safely themselves.
'It's a fairly complex process, but basically...' is your rescue line -- it signals 'here comes the simple version' and puts the listener at ease.
